content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

May 17, 1978

Doris D. Bankus
City Clerk
1700 W. 162nd St.
Gardena, CA 90247

Dear Mrs. Bankus:

The Fair Political Practices Commission has received your letter of
April 18, 1978, and a copy of a letter to you from Mayor Edmond J. Russ.

In view of the March 8, 1978, Appellate Court decision concerning
the threshold for reporting clients by filers in legal or brokerage ser-
vices, you asked if Statements of Economic Interests, Schedule 721-E
(Income of Business Entity which Provides Legal or Brokerage Services),
filed prior to March 8, 1978, are to be treated confidentially. You also
asked if you were correct in assuming that filings made subsequent to
March 8, 1978, must be treated confidentially.

Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) provides that "in the case of
a business entity which provides legal or brokerage services, the name
of every person who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's pro
rata share of fees from such person was equal to or greater than one
thousand dollars ($1,000)" must be disclosed. The recent Appellate Court
decision held that the threshold should be changed from $1,000 to $10,000,
so that a filer need only disclose names of those clients who paid fees
to the business entity of which his pro rata share was $10,000 or more.
However, the Commission's petition appealing this decision has been
accepted by the California Supreme Court, thereby rendering the Appellate
Court decision null and void.

Accordingly, the Commission will continue to enforce the law as
written until such time as the law is stayed by a court order, ruled
invalid by a court or changed by the Legislature. We shall inform per-
sons who decline to comply with Section 87207(b)(2) that they are required
to do so and that noncompliance places them in violation of the Political
Reform Act.

Mrs. Doris D. Bankus
May 17, 1978
Page Two


As a practical matter, we regard Hays v. Wood as a test case with
respect to the constitutionality of Section 87207(b)(2) and, therefore,
have not to this date initiated any other judicial or administrative
enforcement proceedings pending the outcome of that lawsuit. However,
this practice should not be considered tantamount to a policy of non-
enforcement. We still expect public officials with disclosure obliga-
tions under the Act to comply fully. Further, the Commission reserves
the right to initiate other litigation or administrative enforcement
proceedings should it be deemed appropriate in a particular situation.

In light of the fact that Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) is
still in effect, Mr. Russ' present and previous Statements remain public
documents available for public inspection. Therefore, you should not
keep Mr. Russ' Statements confidential nor should you instruct any other
employee or agency to do so.

I hope this has answered your question. If not, please call me
at 916/322-6444 for further clarification.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Ted Prim, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:mfa
cc: Edmond J. Russ,
Mayor, City of Gardena

Michael J. Miller,
City Attorney

EDMOND J. RUSS, Mayor
MAS FUKAI, Mayor Pro Tem
DONALD L. DEAR, Councilman
CHARLES A. NADER, Councilman
VINCENT H. OKAMOTO, Councilman

CITY of GARDENA                1700 WEST 162nd STREET / GARDENA, CALIFORNIA 90247 / (213) 327-0220

DORIS DIAMOND BANKUS, City Clerk
GEORGE KOBAYASHI, City Treasurer
CRAIG A. McDOWELL, Administrative Officer
MICHAEL H. MILLER, City Attorney

April 18, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Legal Division

Gentlemen:

     I have received the attached communication from
Edmond J. Russ, Mayor of the City of Gardena, which you
will find is self-explanatory. Mayor Russ is a lawyer by
profession.

     In view of the Appellate Court decision of March 8,
1978, as outlined in FPPC bulletin dated March 17, 1978,
I request a ruling on the following questions:

     1. Am I correct in assuming that, at least for the
time being, filings made by lawyers and brokers
subsequent to March 8, 1978, must be treated
confidentially?

     2. Are the filings made by attorneys and brokers
prior to March 8, 1978, to be treated confiden-
tially, or are such filings considered public
records?

     3. What is my responsibility as to Mayor Russ' re-
quest that I request any other governing agency or
commission to whom copies may have been sent, in-
cluding the FPPC, to maintain confidentiality as a
result of the above mentioned decision?

2.

4. I have been informed that the FPPC plans to
appeal the March 8 ruling, and if so, would
this not have the effect of staying the ef-
fectiveness of the decision?

Thank you for your response.

Very truly yours,

Doris Diamond Bankus
City Clerk

DDB:pc

Attachment

cc: Mayor Edmond J. Russ
Michael J. Miller, City Attorney

EDMOND J. RUSS, Mayor
CHARLES A. NADER, Mayor Pro Tem
WILLIAM L. (BILL) COX, Councilman
MAS FUKAI, Councilman
VINCENT H. OKAMOTO, Councilman

CITY of GARDENA                1700 WEST 162nd STREET / GARDENA, CALIFORNIA 90247 / (213) 327-0220

CERTIFIED MAIL                                                April 4, 1978
RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED


Mrs. Doris Diamond Bankus
16011 South Denker Avenue
Gardena, CA 90247

Dear Mrs. Bankus:

Re:  Government Code Section 87207, Subdivision (b)(2)
     Hays vs. Wood, 1 Civil 40623, Sup. Ct. #36319
     Filed March 8, 1978

As you may know by this time, the Appellate Court has declared
the above-mentioned provision of the Government Code unconsti-
tutional.

As you also know, I had originally refused to report my clients
who paid a fee of over $1,000 to me; however, because of urging
by the Fair Political Practices Commission, I have reported
those clients for the past several years. I hereby request
that you keep the names of those clients confidential and not
disclose them to public scrutiny.

I also request that you contact any other governing agency or
commission to whom you have sent copies, including the Fair
Political Practices Commission, and request they also maintain
those names confidential as a result of the above-mentioned case.

Thank you for your cooperation and assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Edmond J. Russ
                                        Mayor

EJR:nm

sections.question: 1. Am I correct in assuming that, at least for the time being, filings made by lawyers and brokers subsequent to March 8, 1978, must be treated confidentially? 2. Are the filings made by attorneys and brokers prior to March 8, 1978, to be treated confidentially, or are such filings considered public records? 3. What is my responsibility as to Mayor Russ' request that I request any other governing agency or commission to whom copies may have been sent, including the FPPC, to maintain confidentiality as a result of above mentioned decision? 4. I have been informed that the FPPC plans to appeal the March 8 ruling, and if so, would this not have the effect of staying the effectiveness of the decision?
sections.conclusion: In light of the fact that Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) is still in effect, Mr. Russ' present and previous Statements remain public documents available for public inspection. Therefore, you should not keep Mr. Russ' Statements confidential nor should you instruct any other employee or agency to do so.
sections.question_synthetic: The City Clerk asks whether, following a March 8, 1978 Appellate Court decision changing the client disclosure threshold under Government Code Section 87207(b)(2), statements of economic interests filed by lawyers and brokers should be treated as confidential or public records, and what her obligations are regarding such confidentiality requests.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) remains in full effect because the Commission's petition appealing the Appellate Court decision has been accepted by the California Supreme Court, rendering the Appellate decision null and void. Accordingly, all statements of economic interests filed under this provision remain public documents and must not be kept confidential.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains full question and conclusion sections. Includes background correspondence showing the query origin. The FPPC response clearly states the legal standard remains $1,000 threshold for client disclosure and affirms public record status of statements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Am I correct in assuming that, at least for the time being, filings made by lawyers and brokers subsequent to March 8, 1978, must be treated confidentially? 2. Are the filings made by attorneys and brokers prior to March 8, 1978, to be treated confidentially, or are such filings considered public records? 3. What is my responsibility as to Mayor Russ' request that I request any other governing agency or commission to whom copies may have been sent, including the FPPC, to maintain confidentiality as a result of above mentioned decision? 4. I have been informed that the FPPC plans to appeal the March 8 ruling, and if so, would this not have the effect of staying the effectiveness of the decision?

CONCLUSION: In light of the fact that Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) is still in effect, Mr. Russ' present and previous Statements remain public documents available for public inspection. Therefore, you should not keep Mr. Russ' Statements confidential nor should you instruct any other employee or agency to do so.

You also asked if you were correct in assuming that filings made subsequent to March 8, 1978, must be treated confidentially. Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) provides that "in the case of a business entity which provides legal or brokerage services, the name of every person who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's pro rata share of fees from such person was equal to or greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000)" must be disclosed. Wood as a test case with respect to the constitutionality of Section 87207(b)(2) and, therefore, have not to this date initiated any other judicial or administrative enforcement proceedings pending the outcome of that lawsuit. However, this practice should not be considered tantamount to a policy of non- enforcement. We still expect public officials with disclosure obliga- tions under the Act to comply fully. Further, the Commission reserves the right to initiate other litigation or administrative enforcement proceedings should it be deemed appropriate in a particular situation. In light of the fact that Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) is still in effect, Mr. Russ' present and previous Statements remain public documents available for public inspection. embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a City Clerk's request for guidance on whether economic interest statements of lawyers and brokers should be treated as confidential following a March 8, 1978 Appellate Court decision. The FPPC concludes that the law remains unchanged and enforceable because it has appealed the decision to the California Supreme Court, so all such statements must remain public.
